the time comes and then we're going to tackle a tricky topic around the death of Caroline Flack who is for those of you who aren't in the UK she's a TV presenter who took her own life last week and we want to talk about how this the learning that we can get from this experience is going to help protect you online because we know that many of you are well all of you are business owners who need to get out there and market yourself and actually a lot of you aren't doing it because you worry about the kind of hate that she received online and then there's one other topic isn't there no inaugural show where we're going to make little mistakes like that okay so what are we talking about first we're going into vast area to luging how long have I got so this is such an important topic because this is the time you've got to react to the problem now I often see esthetics practitioners referred to a vascular occlusion as a medical emergency and that often makes you think you've every second counts and

absolutely time does matter but it's important to know exactly how much time and really what we want to know that for us so that you've got the ability to control your actions and put things together in a much more organize the sequel's way without letting panic take over the situation and starting to make bad decisions and because that happens unfortunately all the time people overreact people under react people hide from the problem people think everything's a vascular occlusion so let's talk a little bit about how long it takes from the moment a blood vessel is blocked until the moment you no longer got any chance of reviving that tissue so tape me down take me through because you actually have has an occlusion yourself haven't you take me through the moment it happens and then the process afterwards well the key thing is that the first thing at the moment of diagnosis is quite tricky so a lot of you'd think it's quite simple you know is there blood supply or not but unfortunately mostly these situations are a little bit more complex because you can't tell for sure whether

there's an occlusion as soon as you block a blood vessel the skin doesn't go immediately white and there's often a period where it's in between so you've got a bit of blood flow from before the injection started and then you're it's gradually getting worse over time and the first thing I noticed was as I was injecting I got a little flash and I saw the other parts of the lip just go flash white for a second and then it returned to normal and my initial thought was oh it's nothing so that little spasm but it's not an occlusion then I went back and checked it and it's from that moment where you're basically doing your capillary refill test so we're continuously checking the blood flow which has got a specific technique to it and then even once you've made the decision this is definitely a vascular occlusion then the clock starts ticking how long have you got until that tissue dies and that's the what we're really talking about there is the pathological process of necrosis because there points along the journey of necrosis where it's easily reversible and there are points where it's gone too far and it doesn't matter what you do the tissue is dead so that is your window of opportunity there

are many things apart from how long you've got that can live there can decrease the time between injection and diagnosis and that is a crucial time as a how long between diagnosis and reversal have you got but there's another number which is how long between injection and diagnosis that number we also need to work on shrinking because if you and the little simple things you can do like I've been recommending for years if your you're injecting someone in an area where there's a blood vessel that might get blocked why not just check Polly refill after every procedure so that you've literally go in your mind's eye I checked it was fine and that's actually seen a few clinicians of video it and it becomes extraordinarily useful when they think they've got a problem later on because you've got the original go refill before the hematoma arrived and it makes it a lot simpler to see what and whether or not the blood flow has changed since that moment when you say procedure what do you mean every injection well you don't know you don't have to check after every injection although that would be fine too it's more that when you finish the procedure and you you're about to take your gloves off recheck the blood supply of the face otherwise

you're constantly doing it so finish the procedure and check unless there's some reason why you think you should check sooner like it was an odd injection or it bled or something like that and then presumably another factor in that's going to affect the amount of time you've got ultimately is the information that you give to the patient go away with so safety nothing there yeah so you can minimize that once again you're minimizing the time between occlusion and diagnosis by giving the patient good information so say you just couldn't see on that patient you forgot you know you it wasn't that clear and it they did have an occlusion if they've got adequate information in front of them which is not just what an occlusion is but who to call and what to say and what the signs are you're going to and then you've got planned in place of how you're going to see them quite soon and just and that can be as simple as saying if I ever get someone who I think has this problem they are priority number one and I will see them in my clinic no matter what else is going on or home visit but you're you just does just

another mechanism of minimizing the gap between when the occlusion occurred and when you diagnose it and then you've got when you're diagnosed when you reverse and how long you've got what if they then come back to you let's say they came back to you within three hours and now they're reporting signs of an occlusion is it too late well and first thing is I think you can rely on a committee refill test as a screening tool I think it's fairly unlikely that if you check properly there's some ones can have a big vascular occlusion and you won't notice it I just tell patients the signs and symptoms okay something strange happens or maybe I forget to do well or maybe there's some factor but I bet that I'm not aware of that can decrease the sensitivity but I don't want to diminish that that's an important thing that should reassure you if you've done a good test and you shouldn't immediately think oh it's just a vascular occlusion if quite likely as something else if you've done that test but do check them if in doubt so that the time you've got between the shutting off blood supply and tissue death that's the critical amount of time and

really what that it's really helpful to understand the pathology that's going on the pathogenesis that's the process of disease production almost if you understand that will help you interpret what you're seeing at different points along the journey and it also helps you make decisions about how aggressive you should be in terms of treatment and if you think about what necrosis is it's basically caused by a reduction in blood flow to the skin which means oxygen that's the first and most important nutrient that your body needs all tissues need oxygen but they needed a different amounts depending on how metabolically active they are so if you're talking about skin it has a different metabolic rate to nerves and you know to your neurons in your brain to the retinal artery retinal cells so all of them are slightly different we're mainly talking about skin but there are some tissues which we know a bit more about which can give you an idea of how they're that the metabolic activity affects how long you've got to reverse so and the reason there are different amounts of time is

because each cell is using energy at a different rate and what happens with necrosis is that you're running out of the energy substrate ATP and then there's a knock-on effect as that everything starts to fall apart so you can think about a cell a little bit like a boat that has a permanent hole in it and it's leaking water and there's a machine that's using energy that pumps the water back out and that's your new that boat can carry on floating around endlessly and happily as long as that machine is pumping water out it's not going to sink and that's how you should think about the cells in the face as soon as you shut off the energy supply what happens to their boat well the water starts to spew in and it's not coming out that's exactly what happens actually with a cell when the ATP supply drops and the sodium potassium pump is no able to pump sodium out of the cell and potassium in and it starts to go in the opposite direction which actually means water starts to flow into the cell so the first stage of necrosis is that the cell actually starts to swell up and that's actually easily reversible if you can get the energy back in you're not

going to have a complete necrosis it's the first stage of it but it gets a whole lot Messier after that because and going back to the boat analogy you can imagine that as the water flows up other things are going to start to go wrong like it might affect the electricity supply the boat because the water comes up and it might then flood into the engine and then you're breaking those things it's a little bit like that with cells it there are worsening components as you go up until this completely reversible and the boat sinks so in terms of the cell the that the next thing that happens is you get a UK there is some anaerobic metabolism and that means that you're able to use produce some energy without oxygen but it's really small compared with what you do with oxygen it's about five percent of what the cell needs and that just gives you a little bit of breathing space there's a tiny excuse the pun and there's a tiny bit of time when you're able to this cell is able to keep some of the processes going but it's running out of time you can't run just on an aerobic activity and so the and what happens when you start to run out of those things is the other

organelles start to get damaged so there with the smoother actor-director plasmic smoother reticulum starts to fill up with fluid as well and that starts to expand then you start to get an acid production from the anaerobic production in the cells so that you're producing this acid while you're trying to produce some energy from anaerobic supplies and that starts to distort the enzymes and many of them don't work so well and that's further knocking the cell out of balance and eventually you lose control also of the calcium pumps and then calcium makes a huge difference it starts to activate other enzymes which are really destructive so you start to activate the cells own proteases and nucleases and they basically digest protein you're literally starting the process of the cell accidentally destroying itself to go back to the Bose analogy it's a little bit like maybe the electricity fails and then the engine coolant fan fails when the engine starts to eat up and then a fire breaks out it's that knock-on effect you've now got a fire breaking out effectively inside the cell we is which is starting to destroy little

components of this sales mechanisms which it needs to maintain homeostasis to actually keep itself alive and further on from that you start to destroy the membranes of the lysosomes so lysosomes are little organelles filled with digestive enzymes that your cell would use to basically break down nutrients they've highly destructive and they usually kept safe in these organelles but as soon as you start to break down those organelles you're basically spewing the most toxic stuff just out of control spewing of digestive enzymes everywhere and it that just really starts to destroy everything until the everything including the outer cell membrane breaks down and all the cells constituents just spew out into the surroundings including those enzymes and then you're damaging these cells nearby as well so that not only is that cell that ran out of oxygen being damaged but it's also being attacked by those same out-of-control enzymes the cells next to it start to be attacked so you're basically setting off this there's this chain of chaos where one thing leads to more chaos which leads to more where everything's being destroyed and it's a highly destructive process

because all of the highly tuned processes which your cell has honed over billions of years to keep things in order or break in sequence until there's complete destruction so that's the process of necrosis science there is also but there are times along that journey probably just before the calcium comes into the equation and you start to activate all those destructive enzymes just before that point you might be able to save it now how long that is highly variable they can't give you a precise number but we do know a little bit about different tissues and how long they can last through often through accidents and trauma so if you lose a limb there's a period of window a period of time a window of opportunity where you might be able to reattach the limb without losing tissue and if you take that as an example it's probably between six and eight hours I've seen a few people say six to twelve hours it's not the kind of thing you can run a control trial on there are very little examples very little people who volunteer for that study so it's based on real world experience and they say you know within that time frame
